name: Blackboard Learn REST API
what_it_does: REST and GraphQL API for Anthology (formerly Blackboard) Learn LMS, covering courses, users, content, grades, and groups for institutions serving 100M+ students globally.
use_cases: Institutional reporting and compliance data export
not_for: Greenfield integrations — prefer more modern LMS APIs for new projects
Agents needing predictable API behavior during Anthology Ultra migration
Developers without an existing Blackboard institution relationship
best_when: An institution is already running Blackboard Learn and needs agent-driven automation for existing workflows before or during migration to Anthology Ultra.
avoid_when: Starting a new integration without an existing Blackboard relationship, or when API stability and modern design are priorities.

auth.notes: OAuth 2.0 with 3-legged (user-delegated) and 2-legged (client credentials) flows. Application registration required through Anthology developer portal. Scopes available but not as granular as modern APIs.

pricing.paid_tiers: Institutional licensing via Anthology; pricing not publicly listed
pricing.notes: Enterprise institutional licensing. Developer portal registration is free; API access requires institutional Blackboard Learn deployment.

performance.rate_limits: Rate limits enforced per application; varies by deployment type (SaaS vs self-hosted)

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses are inconsistent between REST and GraphQL surfaces. Legacy error codes from older API versions still appear. Documentation and actual API behavior frequently diverge during the Anthology Ultra migration period.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency support. Agent retries on write operations risk creating duplicates. Agents must implement external state tracking.
known_agent_gotchas: Ongoing migration to Anthology Ultra creates API instability — behavior may change without notice
REST and GraphQL endpoints are not feature-equivalent; some resources only available in one surface
FERPA restrictions apply to all student data access — requires institutional policy compliance
Application registration can take weeks; no self-service sandbox available
Some REST endpoints still exhibit SOAP-era design patterns with deeply nested payloads
Rate limit responses are not consistently documented; agents may hit limits unexpectedly
OAuth token scopes are coarse-grained — hard to build least-privilege agents

security_score_components.security_notes: OAuth 2.0 is present but scope granularity is limited. Legacy architecture means some security patterns are inherited from older Blackboard versions. FERPA compliance at policy level. TLS enforced on SaaS deployments; self-hosted instances vary.
